medical_history.chief_complaint: Loss of muscle tone for a second without observing myoclonic jerks before
medical_history.history_of_present_illness: At age 20 months, the patient's mother noticed that he lost muscle tone for a second without observing myoclonic jerks before. When sitting in a chair, his head might suddenly fall, standing or running, and he would collapse on his knees without an obvious reason. No postictal abnormalities were noticed. The seizures were classified as Atonic Seizures (AtS). They occurred several times during a few days. At the same time after sleep deprivation, he had his first typical Generalized Tonic-Clonic Seizure (GTCS) starting with an initial cry and generalized stiffening followed by clonic convulsive movements lasting 2 to 3 minutes. During that day, he had three more GTCS and was admitted to a pediatric hospital, where six more AtS were reported in the following days. All seizures occurred at various times of the day while the patient was awake. He was successfully treated with phenobarbital. Fever was not reported. He has remained seizure free up to now, although the medication had been stopped at age 4 years.

physical_examination.specialty_examination: Neurologic development was normal except for an isolated retardation of active speech with normal comprehension. Several interictal EEG recordings including a sleep EEG after the first GTCS did not show epileptiform discharges. After medication was stopped, a sleep EEG showed left parietal spikes and one 2-second period of generalized, irregular spike-and-wave discharges of 2.5 to 3 Hz. An MRI scan of the brain at this time was normal and there was no evidence for partial epileptic seizures.

other_tests.findings: Generalized, irregular spike-and-wave discharges of 2.5 to 5 Hz in eight cases and additional focal parietal discharges in one case

One short episode of generalized, irregular spike-and-wave discharges and left-hemispheric focal spikes activated by sleep, presenting as a transverse dipole with a maximum negativity in the left parietal region

diagnosis.final_diagnosis: Generalized epilepsy with febrile seizures plus (GEFS+)
diagnosis.diagnostic_reasoning: The diagnosis was based on the clinical presentation of febrile and afebrile seizures, including generalized tonic-clonic seizures (GTCS), tonic seizures (TS), absence seizures (AbS), and atonic seizures (AtS), within a family showing autosomal dominant inheritance. The age at onset was typically in childhood (mean 2.8 years), and interictal EEG recordings showed generalized spike-and-wave discharges in some cases. The variety of seizure types and the familial pattern fit the spectrum of GEFS+.
